2015.assessment:
Inflation remained at approximately 4.9% in 2015, relatively controlled
despite the economic shock from terrorist attacks. The dinar continued
to depreciate. IMF structural adjustment program maintained some fiscal
and monetary discipline.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2016.assessment:
Inflation eased to approximately 3.7% in 2016, partially reflecting
lower global oil prices. The central bank maintained relatively tight
monetary policy under the IMF program. Economic growth remained slow
but price stability was maintained.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2017.assessment:
Inflation rose to approximately 5.3% in 2017 as dinar depreciation
accelerated and energy subsidies were further cut. IMF austerity
measures put upward pressure on prices. Social discontent over cost
of living increased.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2018.assessment:
Inflation rose sharply to approximately 7.3% in 2018, the highest since
the revolution, driven by dinar depreciation, energy subsidy reduction,
and global commodity prices. January 2018 saw major protests against
the government's budget law and rising costs. The IMF program required
continued austerity.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2019.assessment:
Inflation moderated slightly to approximately 6.7% in 2019 but remained
elevated. The central bank raised interest rates to combat inflation.
The dinar continued to depreciate. Import prices remained high.
Consumer purchasing power continued eroding.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2020.assessment:
Inflation moderated to approximately 5.6% in 2020 as COVID-19 suppressed
demand. Lockdowns reduced consumption and economic activity broadly.
The economy contracted severely (-8.8% real GDP growth). Inflation
remained moderate despite supply chain disruptions.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2021.assessment:
Inflation remained elevated at approximately 5.7% in 2021 as the
economy partially recovered from COVID-19 and global supply chain
pressures emerged. Saied's power grab in July added political uncertainty
but did not immediately change the inflation trajectory. Fiscal pressures
persisted.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2022.assessment:
Inflation surged to approximately 8.3% in 2022, driven by global energy
and food price shocks from the Russia-Ukraine war and continued dinar
depreciation. Tunisia's food import dependence made it particularly
vulnerable to the Ukraine war's commodity price shock. IMF negotiations
for a new program were ongoing but unresolved. Subsidy costs ballooned.
Social pressures from rising prices increased under Saied's rule.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2023.assessment:
Inflation reached approximately 9.3% in 2023, the highest since the
revolution era. Persistent dinar depreciation, continued global price
pressures, and expansionary fiscal policy (despite no IMF program)
sustained elevated inflation. Food shortages of staple goods including
sugar and cooking oil were reported. Saied's government resisted IMF
conditionality for subsidy reform.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, PCPIPCH, Tunisia, April 2025

2024.assessment:
Inflation is estimated to have moderated to approximately 7% in 2024
as global commodity price pressures eased somewhat. However structural
inflation drivers remained — dinar depreciation, subsidy costs, and
fiscal imbalances. No IMF program was in place to discipline monetary
and fiscal policy. Inflation remained well above the historical Ben Ali
era norm.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook Database, estimate, April 2025

1996.assessment:
Tunisia had a highly open economy in 1996 with trade (exports + imports)
representing approximately 93% of GDP. The EU Association Agreement
signed in 1995 was deepening trade integration. Tunisia's export-oriented
manufacturing and tourism sectors drove high trade ratios. Tunisia was
among the most open economies in North Africa and the MENA region.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

1997.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 94% of GDP in 1997. The EU Association
Agreement entered into force in 1998, further deepening the trade
relationship. Tunisia's textile and clothing exports to EU were major
components. The economy was structurally integrated with European markets
and the Ben Ali government actively promoted export-led growth.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

1998.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 93% of GDP in 1998. The EU Association
Agreement was now in force and tariff reductions were beginning to take
effect. Tunisia was navigating the transition from protected markets
to EU competition while maintaining high overall trade integration.
Asian financial crisis reduced some export demand from Asian competitors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

1999.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 90% of GDP in 1999. Global commodity
price weakness and the aftermath of the Asian financial crisis moderated
import costs and some export values. Tunisia maintained its high trade
integration with the EU. The economy remained highly open relative to
regional peers.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2000.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to approximately 95% of GDP in 2000 as global
commodity prices and trade volumes rebounded. Tunisia's EU trade grew
with the recovering European economy. Tourism exports also expanded.
The economy remained one of the most trade-integrated in North Africa.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2001.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 93% of GDP in 2001. The 9/11 attacks
slightly dampened tourism but Tunisia's goods trade with EU remained
strong. Textile and clothing exports were the dominant manufactured
exports. Tunisia was navigating EU competition as tariffs continued
to phase down under the Association Agreement.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2002.assessment:
Trade openness remained approximately 93% of GDP in 2002. The Ghriba
synagogue attack reduced tourism receipts somewhat but did not
fundamentally alter Tunisia's trade integration. Manufacturing exports
compensated. Tunisia maintained its highly open economic model.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
Trade openness grew to approximately 96% of GDP in 2003 as global trade
recovered and Tunisia's EU integration deepened. Tourism began recovering
post-2002 attack. The EU Association Agreement was lowering tariff barriers
progressively. Tunisia's export base was diversifying beyond textiles
into mechanical and electrical components.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
Trade openness approached 100% of GDP in 2004 as both goods and services
trade grew strongly. Tourism receipts grew with record visitor numbers.
EU trade integration was deepening. FDI inflows were also high, supplementing
trade-driven growth. Tunisia's economic openness was near its peak for
the Ben Ali era.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
Trade openness reached approximately 100% of GDP in 2005, reflecting
Tunisia's full integration with EU markets and strong tourism. The
Multifibre Arrangement ending in 2005 created pressure on Tunisian
textile exports from Chinese competition, but other sectors compensated.
Tunisia's open trade model was at its apex.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
Trade openness exceeded 100% of GDP in 2006 for the first time, reaching
approximately 103%. This reflected very strong FDI inflows from privatization
and rising trade volumes. Tunisia was among the most trade-integrated
economies in Africa and the MENA region. The Ben Ali era reached its
peak economic integration with Europe.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
Trade openness reached approximately 104% of GDP in 2007, near the
Ben Ali era peak. Tourism reached near-record levels. EU trade was
growing strongly. Tunisia was deeply integrated with European markets
for both goods (textiles, mechanical parts) and services (tourism,
financial services). The growth model was heavily EU-dependent.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Trade openness reached approximately 105% of GDP in 2008 as high oil
prices increased both export revenues (for energy products) and import
costs. Tunisia's import bill rose sharply with global commodity price
spikes. The EU trade relationship remained the anchor. Despite the
global financial crisis beginning, Tunisia's structural openness
was maintained through the end of 2008.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Tunisia had a highly open economy in 2009 with trade (exports + imports)
representing approximately 99% of GDP. The EU association agreement
provided preferential access to European markets. Tunisia was one of
the most trade-integrated economies in North Africa, with strong textile,
manufacturing, and tourism export sectors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
Trade openness exceeded 100% of GDP in 2010, reflecting Tunisia's deep
integration with European markets. The economy was highly dependent on
EU trade partners (France, Italy, Germany). Textile exports, tourism
earnings, and remittances from the diaspora contributed to the high
openness figure.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
Trade openness declined modestly in 2011 as tourism receipts fell and
some trade was disrupted during the revolution. However, Tunisia's
structural trade integration with Europe was maintained. Textile
exports continued. The decline reflects reduced services trade more
than goods trade disruption.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
Trade openness recovered in 2012 to over 100% of GDP as trade with
Europe resumed its pre-revolution pace. Tunisia maintained its EU
Association Agreement privileges. Import demand for reconstruction
and consumption goods also contributed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
Trade openness remained high at approximately 101% of GDP in 2013.
Tunisia continued integrating with EU markets. The DCFTA (Deep and
Comprehensive Free Trade Area) negotiations with the EU began. Trade
remained the backbone of the Tunisian economy.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
Trade openness remained at approximately 101% of GDP in 2014.
Tunisia's trade structure was maintained through the democratic transition.
EU partnership remained the cornerstone of trade policy.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
Trade openness declined to approximately 99% of GDP in 2015 as tourism
collapsed following the Bardo and Sousse attacks. Services trade fell
sharply while goods trade remained relatively stable. The security
shock reduced tourist arrivals and the associated foreign exchange
earnings.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
Trade openness declined further to approximately 97% of GDP in 2016.
Tourism remained depressed. The dinar's depreciation made imports more
expensive, somewhat compressing import volumes. Tunisia maintained
its open trade regime despite challenges.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to approximately 101% of GDP in 2017.
Export growth improved somewhat. Tunisia maintained its highly open
economic model. The dinar's continued depreciation made exports more
competitive.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
Trade openness reached approximately 106% of GDP in 2018. Tourism
began recovering. Export growth improved as dinar depreciation made
Tunisian goods more competitive. Tunisia maintained its highly open
economic model despite fiscal challenges.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 105% of GDP in 2019 as tourism
recovered further and trade with EU remained strong. Tunisia maintained
its highly open economy. Tourism receipts improved to about $1.7 billion.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
Trade openness fell sharply to approximately 89% of GDP in 2020 as
COVID-19 caused tourism to collapse and global trade flows contracted.
Lockdowns severely disrupted both import and export activity. This was
the lowest trade openness in many years and reflected the global
pandemic shock to an open economy.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to approximately 105% of GDP in 2021 as
global trade rebounded and tourism partially resumed. Tunisia's
structural economic openness was maintained. Saied's political changes
did not immediately affect trade patterns.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
Trade openness was approximately 95% of GDP in 2022. The Russia-Ukraine
war disrupted global supply chains and raised energy and food import
costs. Tunisia's import bill rose substantially. EU trade continued
despite Saied's political changes. The EU-Tunisia migration and
economic cooperation talks continued.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
Trade openness declined to approximately 92% of GDP in 2023. Global
commodity price moderation somewhat reduced both export revenues and
import costs. Tourism recovery continued but did not reach pre-pandemic
levels. EU-Tunisia Comprehensive Partnership signed July 2023 including
economic cooperation components.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2024.assessment:
Trade openness is estimated at approximately 90% of GDP in 2024.
Tunisia maintained its open trade model despite political consolidation
under Saied. EU trade partnership persisted. The structural openness
of the Tunisian economy is durable due to its EU association framework.

sources.citation: IMF/World Bank estimate, accessed 2026-03-01

1996.assessment:
Tunisia's official unemployment rate was approximately 15.8% in 1996,
with persistent structural unemployment despite economic growth. Youth
unemployment was significantly higher, particularly in interior regions.
The Ben Ali government's economic model generated growth but failed
to adequately address the structural mismatch between educated graduates
and available jobs. Official figures likely understated actual joblessness.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

1997.assessment:
Unemployment was approximately 15.4% in 1997. Modest improvement
from the prior year as economic growth accelerated. However, the
labor market structural problems persisted. The formal sector was
insufficient to absorb labor force entrants. Tourism and manufacturing
exports provided some jobs but regional disparities remained large.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

1998.assessment:
Unemployment remained at approximately 15.4% in 1998. The EU Association
Agreement's implementation was expected to create jobs through export
growth but the short-term impact was mixed. Some manufacturing jobs
were lost to competition while new opportunities in export-oriented
sectors were being created. Youth unemployment remained a chronic problem.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

1999.assessment:
Unemployment rose slightly to approximately 15.7% in 1999. Economic
growth was somewhat slower in this period due to global commodity
price weakness. Youth unemployment and educated unemployment remained
severe. The government's official unemployment statistics were
considered an undercount by international observers.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2000.assessment:
Unemployment remained at approximately 15.7% in 2000. Strong economic
growth (~4.7% real) was not translating into proportional job creation
due to the capital-intensive nature of growth in manufacturing exports.
The informal sector absorbed many workers at low productivity. Regional
unemployment in Gafsa and interior areas was much higher than the
national average.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2001.assessment:
Unemployment improved somewhat to approximately 14.9% in 2001. Strong
growth in the prior years and continued tourism expansion created some
employment. However, youth unemployment and the employment gap for
university graduates remained acute. Public sector hiring absorbed
some pressure, setting the stage for later fiscal problems.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2002.assessment:
Unemployment was approximately 14.8% in 2002. The Ghriba attack
impacted tourism employment somewhat but the broader trend was modest
improvement from stronger prior-year growth. The educated unemployed
category was large and growing as university graduation rates increased
faster than formal sector job creation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2003.assessment:
Unemployment declined modestly to approximately 14.5% in 2003 with
the economy growing strongly (~5.5%). Tourism recovered from the 2002
attack. Manufacturing exports to EU created employment. However regional
disparities remained extreme with interior regions having much higher
unemployment than coastal tourism and manufacturing zones.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2004.assessment:
Unemployment improved slightly to approximately 14.2% in 2004 as the
economy grew strongly (~6%). Manufacturing exports and tourism both
expanded. However persistent structural unemployment remained. Youth
unemployment was around 30%. The Ben Ali government claimed success
in job creation while suppressing independent labor reporting.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2005.assessment:
Unemployment held at approximately 14.2% in 2005. Despite continued
economic growth, the labor market was absorbing growing numbers of
graduates with difficulty. The mismatch between the education system
and labor market needs was documented by the World Bank. Regional
disparities continued with interior areas significantly worse.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
Unemployment was approximately 14.3% in 2006. Despite strong growth
(~5.7%), unemployment barely improved due to labor force growth and
structural factors. FDI inflows from privatization were capital-intensive
and did not generate proportional employment. Youth unemployment remained
a chronic concern, particularly for educated youth seeking formal jobs.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
Unemployment declined marginally to approximately 14.1% in 2007 with
the economy at its growth peak (~6.3%). Tourism employment grew.
Manufacturing exports expanded. However structural unemployment
remained deeply embedded. The gap between economic growth and job
creation reflected the exclusionary nature of Ben Ali's growth model.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
Unemployment rose to approximately 14.7% in 2008. The Gafsa mining
protests in early 2008 reflected the desperation of unemployed workers
in interior regions. Global financial crisis effects began appearing
in the second half of 2008. Youth unemployment was estimated at over
30%, particularly among university graduates in interior cities.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Tunisia's official unemployment rate was approximately 13.3% in 2009,
with youth unemployment (15-24) at over 30%. This structural unemployment,
particularly among educated youth in interior regions, was a core
grievance driving the 2011 uprising. The government's reported figure
likely understated actual unemployment through undercounting.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
Unemployment was approximately 13% in 2010, with youth unemployment
remaining persistently high at around 30%. The case of Mohamed Bouazizi,
a fruit vendor who self-immolated after police harassment, symbolized
the broader economic despair of educated unemployed youth. Unemployment
was the central economic grievance of the revolution.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
Unemployment spiked to approximately 18.9% in 2011 as the revolution
disrupted economic activity, tourism collapsed, and FDI fell. The
private sector laid off workers amid the transition uncertainty.
The transitional government faced immediate pressure to create jobs
but had limited resources.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
Unemployment declined slightly to approximately 17.6% in 2012 as the
economy stabilized but remained far above pre-revolution levels.
Youth unemployment remained very high. Interior regions suffered
disproportionately. The troika government struggled to deliver on
economic promises.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
Unemployment declined further to approximately 16.5% in 2013 but
remained structurally elevated. Political instability created economic
uncertainty that dampened private investment and hiring. Regional
disparities between coastal and interior Tunisia persisted.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
Unemployment declined to approximately 15.3% in 2014 following the
successful democratic transition and improved business confidence.
The new constitution and stable political environment allowed some
job creation. However, youth unemployment remained very high at around
30% and interior regions continued to lag.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
Unemployment remained at approximately 15.2% in 2015 despite the
terrorist attacks. Tourism collapse offset growth in other sectors.
Youth unemployment remained around 30%. The IMF program called for
structural reforms to improve labor market functioning.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
Unemployment rose slightly to approximately 15.5% in 2016 as economic
growth remained weak. Ben Guerdane attack and continued security concerns
dampened investment and tourism. Youth unemployment remained around 30%.
IMF austerity measures constrained public sector hiring.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
Unemployment remained persistently at approximately 15.5% in 2017.
Economic growth was insufficient to absorb new labor market entrants.
IMF austerity continued. Tunisia's labor market remained structurally
weak, with large informal sector and high youth and female unemployment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
Unemployment was approximately 15.4% in 2018, essentially flat.
Protests in January 2018 over IMF austerity measures and rising
cost of living highlighted the link between unemployment and social
discontent. The economy was not generating sufficient jobs to reduce
the structural unemployment problem.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
Unemployment improved slightly to approximately 14.9% in 2019.
Tourism recovery contributed. However, youth unemployment remained
very high. Regional disparities persisted. The election of Kais Saied
reflected voter frustration with the economic model that had left
millions unemployed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
Unemployment spiked to approximately 17.8% in 2020 due to COVID-19
pandemic effects. Tourism collapsed, hospitality and services shed jobs,
and lockdowns reduced economic activity sharply. The spike exacerbated
pre-existing structural unemployment and poverty.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
Unemployment declined partially to approximately 16.2% in 2021 as the
economy recovered from COVID-19. However, it remained well above
pre-revolution structural levels. The persistent unemployment was a key
driver of popular support for Saied's July 2021 power grab, as many
Tunisians blamed political parties for failing to address economic problems.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
Unemployment declined to approximately 15.3% in 2022 as post-COVID
recovery continued and tourism further rebounded. However, the structural
unemployment problem under Saied's government remained unaddressed.
No new economic reform agenda was presented. Youth unemployment remained
very high. Saied focused on political consolidation rather than economic reform.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
Unemployment was approximately 15.2% in 2023, with minimal change from
2022. Saied's economic management failed to generate sustained job
creation. The IMF program refusal meant no structural reforms to improve
labor market functioning. Youth unemployment remained a chronic problem
above 35% by some estimates.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2024.assessment:
Unemployment is estimated at approximately 15% in 2024 with little
structural change from prior years. Saied's economic management has not
delivered the job creation needed. Public sector hiring continues but
fiscal constraints limit its scale. Private investment remains subdued
due to political uncertainty and lack of IMF endorsement.

Tunisia participated actively in MENA regional diplomacy and hosted international events. No diplomatic isolation or significant closures.

sources.citation: WTO membership records; UN General Assembly participation

sources.citation: WTO membership records; UN General Assembly participation

2009.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration in 2009. It was a member
of the Arab League, African Union, United Nations, and WTO. The EU
Association Agreement (in force since 1998) provided preferential
relations with Europe. Tunisia had normal relations with all major
powers. It hosted the Arab League headquarters in Tunis.

sources.citation: UN General Assembly membership records, Tunisia

2010.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration through 2010 under Ben Ali. Tunisia was
host of World Social Forum 2010. EU Association Agreement operational.
Relations with France, Italy, US, and Gulf states were strong. Arab
League member. UN Security Council non-permanent member aspirations
were active.

sources.citation: European Commission, EU-Tunisia relations, 2010

2011.assessment:
Following Ben Ali's departure, Tunisia's diplomatic integration improved
qualitatively. The new government received enthusiastic international
support. EU, US, and Gulf states increased engagement. Tunisia became
a model for democratic transition. G8 Deauville Partnership was
established to support Arab Spring transitions including Tunisia.

sources.citation: G8 Deauville Partnership communique, May 2011

2012.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration in 2012. International
support for the democratic transition continued. EU increased financial
assistance. Tunisia maintained membership in all multilateral organizations.
Gulf states provided budget support.

sources.citation: European Commission, EU-Tunisia relations, 2012

2013.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration despite political crisis.
International community supported the National Dialogue process.
IMF program negotiations continued. EU and US expressed confidence
in Tunisia's ability to complete its transition.

sources.citation: US State Department, Tunisia country relations, 2013

2014.assessment:
Tunisia's diplomatic integration deepened in 2014 following successful
elections. International election observers praised the democratic
process. DCFTA negotiations with EU advanced. Expanded bilateral
relations with US, European states, and Gulf countries.

sources.citation: EU Election Observation Mission, Tunisia 2014

2015.assessment:
Tunisia was designated US Major Non-NATO Ally in May 2015, deepening
security cooperation. National Dialogue Quartet won Nobel Peace Prize.
International solidarity after terrorist attacks was strong. Full
diplomatic integration maintained.

sources.citation: US White House, Tunisia Major Non-NATO Ally designation, May 2015

2016.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration in 2016. DCFTA
negotiations with EU continued. IMF EFF program operative. US, EU,
and Gulf security assistance continued. Tunisia hosted international
investment conferences.

sources.citation: European External Action Service, EU-Tunisia relations, 2016

2017.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration in 2017. Multilateral
memberships intact. Bilateral relations strong. IMF program review
proceeded. Tunisia hosted conferences on regional security.

sources.citation: IMF Article IV Consultation, Tunisia, 2017

2018.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration in 2018. Bilateral
investment conference (Tunisia 2020 follow-up). EU and US security
partnerships continued. Full multilateral membership maintained.

sources.citation: IMF Article IV Consultation, Tunisia, 2018

2019.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration in 2019. International
election observation missions deployed for presidential and parliamentary
elections. Credible elections reinforced Tunisia's standing in the
international community.

sources.citation: Carter Center, Tunisia 2019 Election Observation

2020.assessment:
Tunisia maintained full diplomatic integration in 2020. COVID-19
emergency support received from IMF and international donors.
International solidarity with Tunisia's pandemic response. Full
multilateral membership intact.

sources.citation: IMF, Tunisia Rapid Financing Instrument, April 2020

2021.assessment:
Tunisia maintained its formal diplomatic standing in 2021 despite
Saied's power grab. International partners — US, EU, AU — expressed
concern but maintained engagement and did not impose diplomatic isolation.
IMF discussions continued. Tunisia retained full multilateral membership.
The AU condemned the suspension of parliament but did not suspend Tunisia.

sources.citation: African Union, Statement on Tunisia, August 2021
US State Department, Tunisia press briefings, July-October 2021

Tunisia remained an AU, Arab League, WTO, and UN member in good standing. Most countries maintained normal diplomatic relations. Some Western concern over democratic regression but no formal diplomatic downgrading.

2009.assessment:
FDI inflows to Tunisia were approximately 3.4% of GDP in 2009,
reflecting the relatively stable investment climate under Ben Ali.
Tunisia's EU association and pro-business orientation attracted
European manufacturers. However, Trabelsi family cronyism and
corruption limited FDI from reaching its potential. Energy sector
and manufacturing were main FDI recipients.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
FDI inflows were approximately 3.3% of GDP in 2010, similar to 2009.
The investment climate was stable through most of 2010. December
protests reduced investor confidence but too late to affect the 2010
annual figure significantly.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
FDI collapsed to approximately 0.7% of GDP in 2011 following the
revolution. Investors fled political uncertainty; existing projects were
suspended; new commitments were deferred. The sharp decline reflected
the political risk perception during the transitional period.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
FDI partially recovered to approximately 2.3% of GDP in 2012 as
investors returned following the successful October 2011 elections.
The transition to democratic governance improved sentiment. However,
uncertainty about the constitutional process kept FDI below pre-revolution
levels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
FDI remained at approximately 2.2% of GDP in 2013. Political
instability (assassinations, constitutional crisis) dampened investor
confidence. However, the National Dialogue resolution helped restore
some confidence. Energy sector FDI continued.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
FDI improved slightly to approximately 2.5% of GDP in 2014 following
successful democratic elections and new constitution. The improved
political environment attracted more investor interest. Tunisia 2020
investment conference was organized to attract FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
FDI declined slightly to approximately 2.3% of GDP in 2015 following
the Bardo and Sousse attacks. Security concerns dampened investor
confidence particularly in tourism and service sectors. Manufacturing
FDI was less affected.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
FDI remained approximately 2.2% of GDP in 2016. The Tunisia 2020
investment conference in November attracted commitments. Security
situation improved. The IMF EFF program provided some credibility.
FDI remained constrained by high unemployment and fiscal pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
FDI remained approximately 2.3% of GDP in 2017. Tunisia's investment
climate remained constrained by regulatory barriers. Some European
manufacturing investment continued. Dinar depreciation made Tunisia
more competitive for export-oriented FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
FDI improved slightly to approximately 2.5% of GDP in 2018 as tourism
recovery and improved competitiveness attracted some investment. New
investment code reforms helped. However, the business environment
remained challenging.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
FDI was approximately 2.4% of GDP in 2019. The political transition
following Essebsi's death was orderly, maintaining investor confidence.
Energy sector continued to attract investment. FDI remained structurally
at moderate levels.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
FDI dropped sharply to approximately 1.6% of GDP in 2020 due to
COVID-19 pandemic. Global FDI contracted broadly. Tourism and services
investments halted. The pandemic caused investors to defer commitments.
Manufacturing FDI also declined due to supply chain disruptions.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
FDI partially recovered to approximately 2.2% of GDP in 2021 as
global investment rebounded from COVID-19. However, Saied's July
2021 power grab added political uncertainty that dampened some
longer-term investment commitments. Energy sector FDI continued.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
Tunisia had no foreign military presence on its territory in 2009.
Tunisia maintained full military sovereignty. It participated in
UN peacekeeping missions abroad and had bilateral security cooperation
with the US and France, but no foreign bases or troops were stationed
on Tunisian soil. Tunisia was an independent security actor.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2009, Tunisia chapter

2010.assessment:
No foreign military presence in Tunisia in 2010. Full military sovereignty
maintained. Tunisia had security cooperation agreements with US and France
including training and equipment assistance, but no foreign troops
were permanently stationed on Tunisian territory.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2010, Tunisia chapter

2011.assessment:
No foreign military presence in Tunisia during or after the 2011
revolution. The transition was entirely civilian and domestically
managed. No foreign troops were invited in or intervened. Tunisia's
military sovereignty was fully intact throughout the revolution and
transition period.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2011, Tunisia chapter

2012.assessment:
No foreign military presence in Tunisia in 2012. Tunisia maintained
bilateral security assistance relationships with US and France that
included training programs, but no permanent foreign military presence
existed. Tunisia contributed peacekeeping troops to UN missions.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2012, Tunisia chapter

2013.assessment:
No foreign military presence in Tunisia in 2013. Counterterrorism
cooperation with the US intensified following Chaambi insurgency and
the Belaid and Brahmi assassinations. US provided some equipment and
training assistance, but no combat troops were deployed. Military
sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense, security cooperation with Tunisia, 2013

2014.assessment:
No significant foreign military presence in Tunisia. US counterterrorism
assistance continued through training and equipment programs. Tunisia
increased cooperation with France and Italy on border security.
No foreign combat forces on Tunisian soil. Military sovereignty intact.

sources.citation: US Department of Defense, Tunisia security cooperation, 2014

2015.assessment:
Following major terrorist attacks, US and France increased security
assistance including advisors. Major Non-NATO Ally status (May 2015)
enabled increased US military aid. France deployed some training
advisors to assist Tunisian security forces. No foreign combat troops
were based permanently in Tunisia. Military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Tunisia Major Non-NATO Ally, 2015

2016.assessment:
Security assistance from US and France continued including training
advisors and equipment. Tunisia participated in the Global Coalition
Against Daesh. No permanent foreign military bases or combat troops
on Tunisian territory. Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2016, Tunisia chapter

2017.assessment:
Security assistance from Western partners continued. No permanent
foreign combat presence. Tunisia maintained full military sovereignty
while benefiting from bilateral security cooperation programs.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2017, Tunisia chapter

2018.assessment:
Security assistance from US, France, and EU continued. No foreign
combat forces. Tunisia maintained full military sovereignty. Security
forces were increasingly capable of independent operations against
militants.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2018, Tunisia chapter

2019.assessment:
Security cooperation with US, France, and EU continued at lower
intensity as the security situation improved. No foreign military
presence beyond training advisors. Full military sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2019, Tunisia chapter

2020.assessment:
No foreign military presence in Tunisia. Security cooperation with
US and France continued at reduced scale given COVID-19. Full military
sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2020, Tunisia chapter

2021.assessment:
No foreign military presence in Tunisia. Saied's power grab was backed
by the Tunisian military; no foreign forces were involved. Full military
sovereignty maintained throughout 2021.

Tunisia had minimal refugee outflows in 2009. The country was at peace
under Ben Ali with stable if repressive governance. Some political
dissidents emigrated but this did not constitute refugee displacement.
Tunisia hosted some refugees from sub-Saharan Africa transiting to
Europe, but outflows from Tunisia itself were negligible.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2009

2010.assessment:
Minimal refugee outflows from Tunisia in 2010. December 2010 protests
did not trigger displacement. Internally, displaced persons were not
reported. Tunisia remained a transit country for sub-Saharan migrants
heading to Europe.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2010

2011.assessment:
Tunisia's revolution caused minimal direct refugee displacement within
Tunisia. However, the revolution's secondary effect was massive — as
Libya's conflict erupted in February 2011, Tunisia absorbed hundreds
of thousands of Libyan refugees and third-country nationals fleeing
Libya through the Ras Jedir border crossing. Tunisia itself did not
generate significant refugee outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2011

2012.assessment:
Minimal domestic refugee displacement from Tunisia. Some Tunisians
seeking economic opportunities abroad did not qualify as refugees.
Tunisia hosted Libyan refugees and continued as transit for sub-Saharan
Africans. No internal displacement from political violence.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2012

2013.assessment:
Minimal domestic refugee displacement from Tunisia. Chaambi insurgency
displaced some border communities but not at the level of refugee flows.
Tunisia remained a host country for Libyan refugees. Some Tunisian
jihadists traveled to Syria but were not refugees.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2013

2014.assessment:
Minimal domestic refugee flows from Tunisia. Tunisia continued hosting
Libyan refugees; Libya's renewed conflict from mid-2014 added more
Libyan refugees to Tunisia. Tunisian border communities were occasionally
affected by spillover from Libya.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2014

2015.assessment:
Tunisia absorbed Libyan refugees and continued as a destination and
transit country. Domestic displacement from terrorist attacks was
minimal — attacks were concentrated in tourist areas without displacing
Tunisian populations. No significant Tunisian refugee outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2015

2016.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows from Tunisia. Ben Guerdane attack did not
cause population displacement. Libya remained unstable generating
refugees absorbed by Tunisia. No significant Tunisian outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2016

2017.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows from Tunisia. The domestic situation was stable
enough to not generate significant displacement. Tunisia continued
hosting Libyan refugees and sub-Saharan migrants transiting toward
Europe, often via the Mediterranean Sea.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2017

2018.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows from Tunisia. Social protests in January 2018
did not cause displacement. Economic emigration increased but did not
constitute refugee flows. Tunisia continued as a Mediterranean migration
transit country.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2018

2019.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows from Tunisia in 2019. Peaceful elections and
orderly presidential succession reduced any pressure. Economic
emigration toward Europe continued but did not constitute forced
displacement. Tunisia remained a transit country for migrants.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2019

2020.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows from Tunisia. COVID-19 reduced migration and
cross-border movements. Tunisia's COVID-19 response did not generate
displacement. Economic hardship increased but did not cause forced
displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2020

2021.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows from Tunisia following Saied's power grab.
The political changes did not cause mass displacement. Economic
hardship and political uncertainty may have increased economic
emigration, but this did not constitute refugee displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Tunisia, 2021

2022.assessment: Displacement from Tunisia was low in 2022 under Saied. No mass displacement was caused by political persecution, though irregular migration by boat across the Mediterranean intensified due to economic deterioration. This irregular economic migration is distinct from forced displacement/refugee flows. Tunisia also hosted sub-Saharan African migrants in transit. No mass population displacement from Tunisia occurred.

sources.citation: UNHCR Statistics; IOM Tunisia

No mass population displacement from Tunisia occurred.

2009.assessment:
Tunisia faced no international sanctions in 2009. As a US partner
in counterterrorism and a country with an EU Association Agreement,
Tunisia maintained full economic relations with Western powers despite
its authoritarian governance. The Ben Ali government's alignment with
Western security priorities insulated it from sanctions.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Tunisia sanctions list, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
No international sanctions on Tunisia in 2010. Tunisia maintained full
diplomatic and economic relations with EU, US, Arab League members,
and multilateral institutions. The EU Association Agreement remained
fully operative.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Tunisia sanctions list, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
Following the revolution, the EU and US froze assets of Ben Ali and
his associates (targeted individual sanctions on former regime figures).
However, these were not sanctions on Tunisia as a country — they were
targeted sanctions to recover looted assets. Tunisia as a state faced
no economic restrictions and indeed received increased international
support and engagement.

sources.citation: EU Council Decision 2011/72/CFSP freezing assets of Ben Ali associates

2012.assessment:
No country-level sanctions on Tunisia. Targeted asset freezes on Ben
Ali associates continued. Tunisia received international aid and support
for its democratic transition. The EU, US, and Gulf states provided
financial assistance.

sources.citation: EU Council, Tunisia sanctions regime, 2012

2013.assessment:
No country-level sanctions on Tunisia. Targeted sanctions on former
regime officials continued. International support for Tunisia's
democratic transition continued, including IMF program and EU financial
assistance.

sources.citation: EU Council, Tunisia sanctions regime, 2013

2014.assessment:
No sanctions on Tunisia. Targeted sanctions on Ben Ali associates were
gradually lifted as asset recovery proceedings advanced. Tunisia became
a major non-NATO ally of the US in 2015. The country was a model of
democratic transition receiving strong international support.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Tunisia, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
No sanctions on Tunisia. The US designated Tunisia a Major Non-NATO
Ally in 2015, increasing security cooperation. International community
rallied around Tunisia after terrorist attacks with diplomatic and
financial support.

sources.citation: US Department of State, Tunisia Major Non-NATO Ally designation, 2015

2016.assessment:
No sanctions on Tunisia. Tunisia continued receiving international
financial support through IMF program, EU macro-financial assistance,
and US security aid. International financial integration was full.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, accessed 2026-03-01

No sanctions on Tunisia. Tunisia remained a partner of the US, EU, and
international financial institutions. IMF program continued. Full
international economic integration maintained.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, accessed 2026-03-01

No sanctions on Tunisia. Full international economic relations continued.

No sanctions on Tunisia. International engagement remained strong.
